prompt: Extreme macro, museum-grade photograph of a nautilus shell sliced perfectly in half, positioned low and off-center in the left third of the frame. The shell’s spiral reads as time through its natural growth pattern. The inner chambers are pristine, luminous, pearlescent, and warm. Moving outward, a subtle sickly yellow-gray discoloration emerges, spreading unevenly across the next chambers like biological stress before collapse. The outer chambers become chalky, pitted, brittle, and chemically eroded. The final chambers violently disintegrate into debris consisting of fine powder, granular dust, and several large, clearly recognizable broken fragments of the shell’s internal walls. The debris falls downward and further to the bottom-right, forming a structured, layered mound resembling sedimentary accumulation, with subtle density bands indicating increasing volume over time. The deepest part of the rubble is engulfed in near-total darkness, with minimal reflected light, creating a sense of irreversible loss and finality. Lighting transitions from soft warm illumination at the inner spiral to cold gray tones and then deep black toward the outer edge. The upper-right area contains expansive negative space, empty and shadowed, enhancing the sense of collapse into void. No text, no labels, no axes, no infographic styling, no additional objects, no marine animals, no scientific diagrams. Highly realistic texture, emotionally haunting, editorial photography, strong asymmetry, deliberate composition, single frame, elegant and original.
dataset_name: NOAA Ocean Acidification Program — Global Ocean pH Time Series 1985–2024
insight: The spiral’s transition from luminous structure to layered debris makes ocean acidification feel like accelerating, irreversible material collapse over time.
